text: To break the news
Setback
To sugarcoat
Candid
Tactful
To reassure
To push back
To reframe
I'm afraid we've hit a setback -- I can't recommend shipping on Friday.
I completely understand, but I'd rather flag this now than ship a broken release.
What I can do is give you a fix by Wednesday and a new date today.
To be candid, it's safer to wait one day than to corrupt customer data.
Hi Ana. We're still on for the Friday release, right? Marketing has the whole campaign booked for that day.
Thanks for checking, Daniel. I'm afraid we've hit a setback. We found a critical bug on the payment screen, and I can't recommend shipping on Friday.
Ana, we can't move Friday. The campaign goes live that day. Can't we just fix it after launch?
I completely understand the deadline matters, but I'd rather flag this now than ship something that corrupts customer data. I won't overpromise a fix I can't guarantee.
So what am I supposed to tell marketing?
Here's what I can do. We'll have a fix by Wednesday, full testing on Thursday, and a new release Thursday evening -- one day late, not a week.
One day I can work with. I just don't want any surprises.
No surprises. To be candid, I'd rather tell you today than on Friday. The way I see it, we caught a serious problem before your customers did.
I'm afraid we've hit a setback on the payment screen.
I can't recommend shipping on Friday, but here's what I can do.
I completely understand the deadline, but I'd rather flag this now.
Let me reassure you: we have a clear plan to fix this.
The way I see it, we caught a serious problem before your customers did.

Hi Ana, it's Daniel. I wanted to leave you a quick note after our call about the release. Look, I won't pretend I was thrilled to hear it has to slip -- nobody likes moving a date with marketing already booked. But honestly, thank you for being straight with me. You didn't sugarcoat it and you didn't bury it in excuses. You told me exactly what the problem was, you told me you couldn't recommend shipping on Friday, and then you gave me a real plan: a fix by Wednesday and a new release on Thursday. That's the part I appreciate. You pushed back on my deadline, which I did not love in the moment, but you were right, and you never overpromised something you couldn't deliver. The way you put it stuck with me -- we caught a serious problem before our customers did. That's a good way to see it. So here is my one ask: could you send me a short written summary of the new timeline, the fix on Wednesday and the release on Thursday, so I can share it with marketing today? No rush, end of day is fine. Thanks again for handling this the way you did, Ana. Talk soon.

Hey Ana, it's Karen, your team lead. I listened back to your call with Daniel, and I want to give you a bit of coaching, because you handled a genuinely difficult conversation really well. The thing you did brilliantly was stay candid without being blunt. You delivered bad news -- a slipped release, two days out -- and you did not soften it into something meaningless, and you did not drop it like a bomb either. You were honest and you were kind at the same time, and that is exactly the balance we want. I also loved that you pushed back on the Friday deadline instead of just caving to keep him happy. A lot of people overpromise in that moment, and then they miss the new date too, and now they've lost trust twice. You didn't do that. Here is my one tip for next time, and it's a small one. Whenever you deliver bad news, try to hand over the plan in the very same breath. You did this, but lead with it even faster: 'here's the setback, and here's exactly what I'm going to do about it.' When the client hears a path forward at the same time as the problem, they stay calm. And keep reframing the way you did -- catching the bug before customers did is a win, not a failure. That's great instinct. Let's catch up on Friday.

Here is how Ana handled the difficult conversation with the client. First, she broke the news candidly: she told Daniel there was a critical bug and that she could not recommend shipping on Friday. Then, when Daniel pushed for the original date, she pushed back diplomatically and refused to overpromise a fix she could not guarantee. After that, she reassured him with a concrete plan: a fix by Wednesday and a new release on Thursday. Next, she reframed the setback, describing it as catching a serious problem before the customers did. Finally, Daniel thanked her for being candid and straight with him, and the delicate conversation ended well.

To reassure

To overpromise

A critical bug two days before launch is a serious setback.
Ana chose to be candid but tactful when she delivered the news.
She refused to sugarcoat the problem or hide the risk.
To calm the client, she tried to reassure him with a clear plan.
Instead of we failed, she chose to reframe the setback as a quality win.
When Daniel demanded the original date, she calmly pushed back.
I'm afraid we've hit a setback on the payment screen.
I can't recommend shipping on Friday, but here's what I can do.
I completely understand the deadline, but I'd rather flag this now.
To be candid, it's safer to wait one day than to corrupt customer data.
What I can do is give you a fix by Wednesday and a new date today.
Let me reassure you: we have a clear plan to fix this.
The way I see it, we caught a serious problem before your customers did.
